question:
Let
\[
P_n(x) = 1 + 2 x  + 3 x^2 + \cdots + n x^{n-1}.
\]
Prove that the polynomials $P_j(x)$
and $P_k(x)$ are relatively prime
for all positive integers $j$ and $k$ with $j \neq k$.
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose to the contrary that there exist positive integers $i \neq j$ and a complex number $z$ such that $P_i(z) = P_j(z) = 0$. Note that $z$ cannot be a nonnegative real number or else $P_i(z), P_j(z) > 0$; we may put $w = z^{-1} \neq 0,1$. For $n \in \{i+1,j+1\}$ we compute that
\[
w^n = n w - n + 1,
\qquad \overline{w}^n =  n \overline{w} - n + 1;
\]
note crucially that these equations also hold for $n \in \{0,1\}$.
Therefore, the function $f: [0, +\infty) \to \RR$ given by
\[
f(t) = \left| w \right|^{2t} - t^2 \left| w \right|^2 + 2t(t-1)\mathrm{Re}(w) - (t-1)^2
\]
satisfies $f(t) = 0$ for $t \in \{0,1,i+1,j+1\}$. On the other hand, for all $t \geq 0$ we have
\[
f'''(t) = (2 \log \left| w \right|)^3 \left| w \right|^{2t} > 0,
\]
so by Rolle's theorem, the equation $f^{(3-k)}(t) = 0$ has at most $k$ distinct solutions for $k=0,1,2,3$. This yields the desired contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By similar reasoning, an equation of the form $e^{x} = P(x)$ in which $P$ is a real polynomial of degree $d$ has at most $d+1$ real solutions. This turns out to be closely related to a concept in mathematical logic known as \emph{o-minimality}, which in turn has deep consequences for the solution of Diophantine equations.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
\setcounter{lemma}{0}
(by Noam Elkies)
We recall a result commonly known as the \emph{Enestr\"om-Kakeya theorem}.
\begin{lemma}
Let 
\[
f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + \cdots + a_n x^n
\]
be a polynomial with real coefficients such that $0 < a_0 \leq a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_n$.
Then every root $z \in \CC$ of $f$ satisfies $|z| \leq 1$.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
If $f(z) = 0$, then we may rearrange the equality $0 = f(z)(z-1)$ to obtain
\[
a_n z^{n+1} = (a_n - a_{n-1}) z^n + \cdots + (a_1 - a_0)z + a_0.
\]
But if $|z| > 1$, then 
\[
|a_n z^{n+1}| \leq (|a_n - a_{n-1}| + \cdots + |a_1 - a_0|) |z|^{n}
\leq |a_n z^{n}|,
\]
contradiction.
\end{proof}
\begin{cor}
Let 
\[
f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + \cdots + a_n x^n
\]
be a polynomial with positive real coefficients. Then every root $z \in \CC$ of $f$ satisfies $r \leq |z| \leq R$ for
\begin{align*}
r &= \min\{a_0/a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}/a_n\} \\
R &= \max\{a_0/a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}/a_n\}.
\end{align*}
\end{cor}
\begin{proof}
The bound $|z| \leq R$ follows by applying the lemma to the polynomial $f(x/R)$.
The bound $|z| \geq r$ follows by applying the lemma to the reverse of the polynomial $f(x/r)$.
\end{proof}
Suppose now that $P_i(z) = P_j(z) = 0$ for some $z \in \CC$ and some integers $i < j$. 
We clearly cannot have $j = i+1$, as then $P_i(0) \neq 0$ and so $P_j(z) - P_i(z) = (i+1) z^i \neq 0$; we thus have $j-i \geq 2$.
By applying Corollary~2 to $P_i(x)$, we see that $|z| \leq 1 - \frac{1}{i}$. On the other hand, by applying Corollary~2 to $(P_j(x) - P_i(x))/x^{i-1}$, we see that $|z| \geq 1 - \frac{1}{i+2}$, contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:} Elkies also reports that this problem is his submission, dating back to 2005 and arising from work of Joe Harris.
It dates back further to Example 3.7 in: Hajime Kaji,
On the tangentially degenerate curves,
\textit{J. London Math. Soc. (2)} \textbf{33} (1986), 430--440, in which the second solution is given.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Elkies points out a mild generalization which may be treated using the first solution but not the second: for integers $a<b<c<d$ and $z \in \CC$
which is neither zero nor a root of unity, the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
a & b & c & d \\
z^a & z^b & z^c & z^d
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has rank 3 (the problem at hand being the case $a=0, b=1, c=i+1, d=j+1$).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It seems likely that the individual polynomials $P_k(x)$ are all irreducible, but this appears difficult to prove.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by David Feldman)
Note that
\[
P_n(x)(1-x) = 1 + x + \cdots + x^{n-1} - nx^n.
\]
If $|z| \geq 1$, then
\[
n|z|^n \geq |z|^{n-1} + \cdots + 1 \geq |z^{n-1} + \cdots + 1|,
\]
with the first equality occurring only if $|z| = 1$ and the second equality occurring only if $z$ is a positive real number. Hence the equation $P_n(z)(1-z) = 0$ has no solutions  with $|z| \geq 1$ other than the trivial solution $z=1$. Since
\[
P_n(x)(1-x)^2 = 1 - (n+1) x^n + nx^{n+1},
\]
it now suffices to check that the curves
\[
C_n = \{ z \in \CC: 0 < |z| < 1, \left| z \right|^n \left| n+1 - zn \right| = 1 \}
\]
are pairwise disjoint as $n$ varies over positive integers.

Write $z = u+iv$; we may assume without loss of generality that $v \geq 0$.
Define the function
\[
E_z(n) = n \log |z| + \log |n+1-zn|.
\]
One computes that for $n \in \RR$, $E_z''(n) < 0$ if and only if
\[
\frac{u-v-1}{(1-u)^2 + v^2} < n <
\frac{u+v-1}{(1-u)^2 + v^2}.
\]
In addition, $E_z(0) = 0$ and 
\[
E_z'(0) = \frac{1}{2} \log (u^2+v^2) + (1-u) \geq \log (u) + 1 - u \geq 0
\]
since $\log(u)$ is concave. From this, it follows that the equation $E_z(n) = 0$
can have at most one solution with $n>0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The reader may notice a strong similarity between this solution and the first solution. The primary difference is we compute that $E'_z(0) \geq 0$ instead of discovering that $E_z(-1) = 0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It is also possible to solve this problem using a $p$-adic valuation on the field of algebraic numbers in place of the complex absolute value; however, this leads to a substantially more complicated solution. In lieu of including such a solution here,
we refer to the approach described by Victor Wang 
here: \url{http://www.artofproblemsolving.com/Forum/viewtopic.php?f=80&t=616731}.

descriptive_long.question:
Let
\[
polyindexn(variablex) = 1 + 2 \, variablex  + 3 \, variablex^2 + \cdots + indexn \, variablex^{indexn-1}.
\]
Prove that the polynomials $polyindexj(variablex)$
and $polyindexk(variablex)$ are relatively prime
for all positive integers $indexj$ and $indexk$ with $indexj \neq indexk$.
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose to the contrary that there exist positive integers $indexi \neq indexj$ and a complex number $complexz$ such that $polyindexi(complexz) = polyindexj(complexz) = 0$. Note that $complexz$ cannot be a nonnegative real number or else $polyindexi(complexz), polyindexj(complexz) > 0$; we may put $inversez = complexz^{-1} \neq 0,1$. For $indexn \in \{indexi+1,indexj+1\}$ we compute that
\[
inversez^{indexn} = indexn \, inversez - indexn + 1,
\qquad \overline{inversez}^{indexn} =  indexn \, \overline{inversez} - indexn + 1;
\]
note crucially that these equations also hold for $indexn \in \{0,1\}$.  Therefore, the function $funcmain: [0, +\infty) \to \RR$ given by
\[
funcmain(variablet) = \left| inversez \right|^{2variablet} - variablet^2 \left| inversez \right|^2 + 2variablet(variablet-1)\mathrm{Re}(inversez) - (variablet-1)^2
\]
satisfies $funcmain(variablet) = 0$ for $variablet \in \{0,1,indexi+1,indexj+1\}$. On the other hand, for all $variablet \geq 0$ we have
\[
funcmain'''(variablet) = (2 \log \left| inversez \right|)^3 \left| inversez \right|^{2variablet} > 0,
\]
so by Rolle's theorem, the equation $funcmain^{(3-indexk)}(variablet) = 0$ has at most $indexk$ distinct solutions for $indexk=0,1,2,3$. This yields the desired contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By similar reasoning, an equation of the form $e^{variablex} = P(variablex)$ in which $P$ is a real polynomial of degree $d$ has at most $d+1$ real solutions. This turns out to be closely related to a concept in mathematical logic known as \emph{o-minimality}, which in turn has deep consequences for the solution of Diophantine equations.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Noam Elkies)
We recall a result commonly known as the \emph{Enestr\"om-Kakeya theorem}.
\begin{lemma}
Let 
\[
funcmain(variablex) = coeffazero + coeffaone \, variablex + \cdots + coeffan \, variablex^{indexn}
\]
be a polynomial with real coefficients such that $0 < coeffazero \leq coeffaone \leq \cdots \leq coeffan$.
Then every root $complexz \in \CC$ of $funcmain$ satisfies $|complexz| \leq 1$.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
If $funcmain(complexz) = 0$, then we may rearrange the equality $0 = funcmain(complexz)(complexz-1)$ to obtain
\[
coeffan \, complexz^{indexn+1} = (coeffan - coeffaminus) \, complexz^{indexn} + \cdots + (coeffaone - coeffazero)complexz + coeffazero.
\]
But if $|complexz| > 1$, then 
\[
|coeffan \, complexz^{indexn+1}| \leq (|coeffan - coeffaminus| + \cdots + |coeffaone - coeffazero|) |complexz|^{indexn}
\leq |coeffan \, complexz^{indexn}|,
\]
contradiction.
\end{proof}
\begin{cor}
Let 
\[
funcmain(variablex) = coeffazero + coeffaone \, variablex + \cdots + coeffan \, variablex^{indexn}
\]
be a polynomial with positive real coefficients. Then every root $complexz \in \CC$ of $funcmain$ satisfies $radiuslo \leq |complexz| \leq radiusrr$ for
\[
radiuslo = \min\{coeffazero/coeffaone, \dots, coeffaminus/coeffan\}, \qquad
radiusrr = \max\{coeffazero/coeffaone, \dots, coeffaminus/coeffan\}.
\]
\end{cor}
\begin{proof}
The bound $|complexz| \leq radiusrr$ follows by applying the lemma to the polynomial $funcmain(variablex/radiusrr)$.
The bound $|complexz| \geq radiuslo$ follows by applying the lemma to the reverse of the polynomial $funcmain(variablex/radiuslo)$.
\end{proof}
Suppose now that $polyindexi(complexz) = polyindexj(complexz) = 0$ for some $complexz \in \CC$ and some integers $indexi < indexj$. 
We clearly cannot have $indexj = indexi+1$, as then $polyindexi(0) \neq 0$ and so $polyindexj(complexz) - polyindexi(complexz) = (indexi+1) \, complexz^{indexi} \neq 0$; we thus have $indexj-indexi \geq 2$.
By applying Corollary~2 to $polyindexi(variablex)$, we see that $|complexz| \leq 1 - \frac{1}{indexi}$. On the other hand, by applying Corollary~2 to $(polyindexj(variablex) - polyindexi(variablex))/variablex^{indexi-1}$, we see that $|complexz| \geq 1 - \frac{1}{indexi+2}$, contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:} Elkies also reports that this problem is his submission, dating back to 2005 and arising from work of Joe Harris.
It dates back further to Example 3.7 in: Hajime Kaji,
\emph{On the tangentially degenerate curves},
\textit{J. London Math. Soc. (2)} \textbf{33} (1986), 430--440, in which the second solution is given.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Elkies points out a mild generalization which may be treated using the first solution but not the second: for integers indexa<indexb<indexc<indexd and $complexz \in \CC$
which is neither zero nor a root of unity, the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
indexa & indexb & indexc & indexd \\
complexz^{indexa} & complexz^{indexb} & complexz^{indexc} & complexz^{indexd}
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has rank 3 (the problem at hand being the case indexa=0, indexb=1, indexc=indexi+1, indexd=indexj+1).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It seems likely that the individual polynomials $polyindexk(variablex)$ are all irreducible, but this appears difficult to prove.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by David Feldman)
Note that
\[
polyindexn(variablex)(1-variablex) = 1 + variablex + \cdots + variablex^{indexn-1} - indexn\,variablex^{indexn}.
\]
If $|complexz| \geq 1$, then
\[
indexn|complexz|^{indexn} \geq |complexz|^{indexn-1} + \cdots + 1 \geq |complexz^{indexn-1} + \cdots + 1|,
\]
with the first equality occurring only if $|complexz| = 1$ and the second equality occurring only if $complexz$ is a positive real number. Hence the equation $polyindexn(complexz)(1-complexz) = 0$ has no solutions  with $|complexz| \geq 1$ other than the trivial solution $complexz=1$. Since
\[
polyindexn(variablex)(1-variablex)^2 = 1 - (indexn+1) \, variablex^{indexn} + indexn\,variablex^{indexn+1},
\]
it now suffices to check that the curves
\[
curvefamily = \{ complexz \in \CC: 0 < |complexz| < 1, | complexz |^{indexn} \, | indexn+1 - complexz\,indexn | = 1 \}
\]
are pairwise disjoint as $indexn$ varies over positive integers.

Write $complexz = realpart+ i\,imagpart$; we may assume without loss of generality that $imagpart \geq 0$.
Define the function
\[
energyfunc_{complexz}(indexn) = indexn \log |complexz| + \log |indexn+1-complexz\,indexn|.
\]
One computes that for $indexn \in \RR$, $energyfunc_{complexz}''(indexn) < 0$ if and only if
\[
\frac{realpart-imagpart-1}{(1-realpart)^2 + imagpart^2} < indexn <
\frac{realpart+imagpart-1}{(1-realpart)^2 + imagpart^2}.
\]
In addition, $energyfunc_{complexz}(0) = 0$ and 
\[
energyfunc_{complexz}'(0) = \frac{1}{2} \log (realpart^2+imagpart^2) + (1-realpart) \geq \log (realpart) + 1 - realpart \geq 0
\]
since $\log(realpart)$ is concave. From this, it follows that the equation $energyfunc_{complexz}(indexn) = 0$
can have at most one solution with $indexn>0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The reader may notice a strong similarity between this solution and the first solution. The primary difference is we compute that $energyfunc'_{complexz}(0) \geq 0$ instead of discovering that $energyfunc_{complexz}(-1) = 0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It is also possible to solve this problem using a $p$-adic valuation on the field of algebraic numbers in place of the complex absolute value; however, this leads to a substantially more complicated solution. In lieu of including such a solution here,
we refer to the approach described by Victor Wang 
here: http://www.artofproblemsolving.com/Forum/viewtopic.php?f=80&t=616731.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let
\[
moonlitshore(ambergrisresin) = 1 + 2 ambergrisresin  + 3 ambergrisresin^2 + \cdots + thunderstrike ambergrisresin^{thunderstrike-1}.
\]
Prove that the polynomials $driftwoodlog(ambergrisresin)$
and $honeyedmead(ambergrisresin)$ are relatively prime
for all positive integers parchmentquill and foxglovelure with parchmentquill \neq foxglovelure.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose to the contrary that there exist positive integers $i \neq parchmentquill$ and a complex number $willowbranch$ such that $sandcastles(willowbranch) = driftwoodlog(willowbranch) = 0$. Note that $willowbranch$ cannot be a nonnegative real number or else $sandcastles(willowbranch),\ driftwoodlog(willowbranch) > 0$; we may put $parchmentroll = willowbranch^{-1} \neq 0,1$. For $thunderstrike \in \{i+1,parchmentquill+1\}$ we compute that
\[
parchmentroll^{thunderstrike} = thunderstrike \, parchmentroll - thunderstrike + 1,
\qquad \overline{parchmentroll}^{\,\thunderstrike} =  thunderstrike \, \overline{parchmentroll} - thunderstrike + 1;
\]
note crucially that these equations also hold for $thunderstrike \in \{0,1\}$. Therefore, the function $kaleidoscope: [0, +\infty) \to \RR$ given by
\[
kaleidoscope(lanternshade) = \left| parchmentroll \right|^{2\,lanternshade} - lanternshade^2 \left| parchmentroll \right|^2 + 2\,lanternshade(lanternshade-1)\mathrm{Re}(parchmentroll) - (lanternshade-1)^2
\]
satisfies $kaleidoscope(lanternshade) = 0$ for $lanternshade \in \{0,1,i+1,parchmentquill+1\}$. On the other hand, for all $lanternshade \geq 0$ we have
\[
kaleidoscope'''(lanternshade) = (2 \log \left| parchmentroll \right|)^3 \left| parchmentroll \right|^{2\,lanternshade} > 0,
\]
so by Rolle's theorem, the equation $kaleidoscope^{(3-foxglovelure)}(lanternshade) = 0$ has at most $foxglovelure$ distinct solutions for $foxglovelure=0,1,2,3$. This yields the desired contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By similar reasoning, an equation of the form $e^{x} = P(x)$ in which $P$ is a real polynomial of degree $d$ has at most $d+1$ real solutions. This turns out to be closely related to a concept in mathematical logic known as \emph{o-minimality}, which in turn has deep consequences for the solution of Diophantine equations.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
\setcounter{lemma}{0}
(by Noam Elkies)
We recall a result commonly known as the \emph{Enestr"om-Kakeya theorem}.
\begin{lemma}
Let 
\[
kaleidoscope(ambergrisresin) = hazelgrouse + emberfirefly \, ambergrisresin + \cdots + cedarwoodmist \, ambergrisresin^{thunderstrike}
\]
be a polynomial with real coefficients such that $0 < hazelgrouse \leq emberfirefly \leq \cdots \leq cedarwoodmist$.
Then every root $willowbranch \in \CC$ of $kaleidoscope$ satisfies $|willowbranch| \leq 1$.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
If $kaleidoscope(willowbranch) = 0$, then we may rearrange the equality $0 = kaleidoscope(willowbranch)(willowbranch-1)$ to obtain
\[
cedarwoodmist \, willowbranch^{thunderstrike+1} = (cedarwoodmist - sparrowfeather) \, willowbranch^{\thunderstrike} + \cdots + (emberfirefly - hazelgrouse) \, willowbranch + hazelgrouse.
\]
But if $|willowbranch| > 1$, then 
\[
|cedarwoodmist \, willowbranch^{\thunderstrike+1}| \leq (|cedarwoodmist - sparrowfeather| + \cdots + |emberfirefly - hazelgrouse|) |willowbranch|^{\thunderstrike}
\leq |cedarwoodmist \, willowbranch^{\thunderstrike}|,
\]
contradiction.
\end{proof}
\begin{cor}
Let 
\[
kaleidoscope(ambergrisresin) = hazelgrouse + emberfirefly \, ambergrisresin + \cdots + cedarwoodmist \, ambergrisresin^{\thunderstrike}
\]
be a polynomial with positive real coefficients. Then every root $willowbranch \in \CC$ of $kaleidoscope$ satisfies $marigoldbloom \leq |willowbranch| \leq pineconess$ for
\begin{align*}
marigoldbloom &= \min\{hazelgrouse/emberfirefly, \dots, sparrowfeather/cedarwoodmist\} \\
pineconess &= \max\{hazelgrouse/emberfirefly, \dots, sparrowfeather/cedarwoodmist\}.
\end{align*}
\end{cor}
\begin{proof}
The bound $|willowbranch| \leq pineconess$ follows by applying the lemma to the polynomial $kaleidoscope(ambergrisresin/pineconess)$.
The bound $|willowbranch| \geq marigoldbloom$ follows by applying the lemma to the reverse of the polynomial $kaleidoscope(ambergrisresin/marigoldbloom)$.
\end{proof}
Suppose now that $sandcastles(willowbranch) = driftwoodlog(willowbranch) = 0$ for some $willowbranch \in \CC$ and some integers $i < parchmentquill$. 
We clearly cannot have $parchmentquill = i+1$, as then $sandcastles(0) \neq 0$ and so $driftwoodlog(willowbranch) - sandcastles(willowbranch) = (i+1) \, willowbranch^i \neq 0$; we thus have $parchmentquill - i \geq 2$.
By applying Corollary~2 to $sandcastles(ambergrisresin)$, we see that $|willowbranch| \leq 1 - \frac{1}{i}$. On the other hand, by applying Corollary~2 to $(driftwoodlog(ambergrisresin) - sandcastles(ambergrisresin))/ambergrisresin^{\,i-1}$, we see that $|willowbranch| \geq 1 - \frac{1}{i+2}$, contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:} Elkies also reports that this problem is his submission, dating back to 2005 and arising from work of Joe Harris.
It dates back further to Example 3.7 in: Hajime Kaji,
On the tangentially degenerate curves,
\textit{J. London Math. Soc. (2)} \textbf{33} (1986), 430--440, in which the second solution is given.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Elkies points out a mild generalization which may be treated using the first solution but not the second: for integers crystalflame<riveroctopus<cloudberryjam<stardustveil and willowbranch \in \CC
which is neither zero nor a root of unity, the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
crystalflame & riveroctopus & cloudberryjam & stardustveil \\
willowbranch^{crystalflame} & willowbranch^{riveroctopus} & willowbranch^{cloudberryjam} & willowbranch^{stardustveil}
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has rank 3 (the problem at hand being the case $crystalflame=0, riveroctopus=1, cloudberryjam=i+1, stardustveil=parchmentquill+1$).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It seems likely that the individual polynomials $P_k(x)$ are all irreducible, but this appears difficult to prove.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by David Feldman)
Note that
\[
moonlitshore(ambergrisresin)(1-ambergrisresin) = 1 + ambergrisresin + \cdots + ambergrisresin^{\thunderstrike-1} - \thunderstrike\, ambergrisresin^{\thunderstrike}.
\]
If $|willowbranch| \geq 1$, then
\[
\thunderstrike |willowbranch|^{\thunderstrike} \geq |willowbranch|^{\thunderstrike-1} + \cdots + 1 \geq |willowbranch^{\thunderstrike-1} + \cdots + 1|,
\]
with the first equality occurring only if $|willowbranch| = 1$ and the second equality occurring only if $willowbranch$ is a positive real number. Hence the equation $moonlitshore(willowbranch)(1-willowbranch) = 0$ has no solutions  with $|willowbranch| \geq 1$ other than the trivial solution $willowbranch=1$. Since
\[
moonlitshore(ambergrisresin)(1-ambergrisresin)^2 = 1 - (\thunderstrike+1) \, ambergrisresin^{\thunderstrike} + \thunderstrike \, ambergrisresin^{\thunderstrike+1},
\]
it now suffices to check that the curves
\[
orchardleaf = \{ willowbranch \in \CC: 0 < |willowbranch| < 1, \left| willowbranch \right|^{\thunderstrike} \left| \thunderstrike+1 - willowbranch \,\thunderstrike \right| = 1 \}
\]
are pairwise disjoint as $\thunderstrike$ varies over positive integers.

Write $willowbranch = silverspoon + i thistlebrush$; we may assume without loss of generality that $thistlebrush \geq 0$.
Define the function
\[
daisyfield(\thunderstrike) = \thunderstrike \log |willowbranch| + \log |\thunderstrike+1-willowbranch\, \thunderstrike|.
\]
One computes that for $\thunderstrike \in \RR$, $daisyfield''(\thunderstrike) < 0$ if and only if
\[
\frac{silverspoon - thistlebrush - 1}{(1 - silverspoon)^2 + thistlebrush^2} < \thunderstrike <
\frac{silverspoon + thistlebrush - 1}{(1 - silverspoon)^2 + thistlebrush^2}.
\]
In addition, $daisyfield(0) = 0$ and 
\[
daisyfield'(0) = \frac{1}{2} \log (silverspoon^2+thistlebrush^2) + (1-silverspoon) \geq \log (silverspoon) + 1 - silverspoon \geq 0
\]
since $\log(silverspoon)$ is concave. From this, it follows that the equation $daisyfield(\thunderstrike) = 0$
can have at most one solution with $\thunderstrike>0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The reader may notice a strong similarity between this solution and the first solution. The primary difference is we compute that $daisyfield'(0) \geq 0$ instead of discovering that $daisyfield(-1) = 0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It is also possible to solve this problem using a $p$-adic valuation on the field of algebraic numbers in place of the complex absolute value; however, this leads to a substantially more complicated solution. In lieu of including such a solution here,
we refer to the approach described by Victor Wang 
here: \url{http://www.artofproblemsolving.com/Forum/viewtopic.php?f=80&t=616731}.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let
\[
transientsum(outputval) = 1 + 2 outputval  + 3 outputval^2 + \cdots + limitless outputval^{limitless-1}.
\]
Prove that the polynomials $transienttwo(outputval)$
and $transienttri(outputval)$ are relatively prime
for all positive integers $discrete$ and $mysterious$ with $discrete \neq mysterious$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose to the contrary that there exist positive integers $continuous \neq discrete$ and a complex number $realnumber$ such that $transientone(realnumber) = transienttwo(realnumber) = 0$. Note that $realnumber$ cannot be a nonnegative real number or else $transientone(realnumber), transienttwo(realnumber) > 0$; we may put $immutable = realnumber^{-1} \neq 0,1$. For $limitless \in \{continuous+1,discrete+1\}$ we compute that
\[
immutable^{limitless} = limitless\, immutable - limitless + 1,
\qquad \overline{immutable}^{limitless} =  limitless\, \overline{immutable} - limitless + 1;
\]
note crucially that these equations also hold for $limitless \in \{0,1\}$. Therefore, the function $constant: [0, +\infty) \to \RR$ given by
\[
constant(stationary) = \left| immutable \right|^{2stationary} - stationary^2 \left| immutable \right|^2 + 2stationary(stationary-1)\mathrm{Re}(immutable) - (stationary-1)^2
\]
satisfies $constant(stationary) = 0$ for $stationary \in \{0,1,continuous+1,discrete+1\}$. On the other hand, for all $stationary \geq 0$ we have
\[
constant'''(stationary) = (2 \log \left| immutable \right|)^3 \left| immutable \right|^{2stationary} > 0,
\]
so by Rolle's theorem, the equation $constant^{(3-mysterious)}(stationary) = 0$ has at most $mysterious$ distinct solutions for $mysterious=0,1,2,3$. This yields the desired contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By similar reasoning, an equation of the form $e^{outputval} = P(outputval)$ in which $P$ is a real polynomial of degree $deltaaxis$ has at most $deltaaxis+1$ real solutions. This turns out to be closely related to a concept in mathematical logic known as \emph{o-minimality}, which in turn has deep consequences for the solution of Diophantine equations.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
\setcounter{lemma}{0}
(by Noam Elkies)
We recall a result commonly known as the \emph{Enestr"om-Kakeya theorem}.
\begin{lemma}
Let 
\[
constant(outputval) = shiftprime + shiftsecond\, outputval + \cdots + shiftfinal\, outputval^{limitless}
\]
be a polynomial with real coefficients such that $0 < shiftprime \leq shiftsecond \leq \cdots \leq shiftfinal$.
Then every root $realnumber \in \CC$ of $constant$ satisfies $|realnumber| \leq 1$.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
If $constant(realnumber) = 0$, then we may rearrange the equality $0 = constant(realnumber)(realnumber-1)$ to obtain
\[
shiftfinal\, realnumber^{limitless+1} = (shiftfinal - shiftpenult)\, realnumber^{limitless} + \cdots + (shiftsecond - shiftprime)realnumber + shiftprime.
\]
But if $|realnumber| > 1$, then 
\[
|shiftfinal\, realnumber^{limitless+1}| \leq (|shiftfinal - shiftpenult| + \cdots + |shiftsecond - shiftprime|) |realnumber|^{limitless}
\leq |shiftfinal\, realnumber^{limitless}|,
\]
contradiction.
\end{proof}
\begin{cor}
Let 
\[
constant(outputval) = shiftprime + shiftsecond\, outputval + \cdots + shiftfinal\, outputval^{limitless}
\]
be a polynomial with positive real coefficients. Then every root $realnumber \in \CC$ of $constant$ satisfies $diameter \leq |realnumber| \leq centerpoint$ for
\begin{align*}
diameter &= \min\{shiftprime/shiftsecond, \dots, shiftpenult/shiftfinal\} \\
centerpoint &= \max\{shiftprime/shiftsecond, \dots, shiftpenult/shiftfinal\}.
\end{align*}
\end{cor}
\begin{proof}
The bound $|realnumber| \leq centerpoint$ follows by applying the lemma to the polynomial $constant(outputval/centerpoint)$.
The bound $|realnumber| \geq diameter$ follows by applying the lemma to the reverse of the polynomial $constant(outputval/diameter)$.
\end{proof}
Suppose now that $transientone(realnumber) = transienttwo(realnumber) = 0$ for some $realnumber \in \CC$ and some integers $continuous < discrete$. 
We clearly cannot have $discrete = continuous+1$, as then $transientone(0) \neq 0$ and so $transienttwo(realnumber) - transientone(realnumber) = (continuous+1) realnumber^{continuous} \neq 0$; we thus have $discrete-continuous \geq 2$.
By applying Corollary~2 to $transientone(outputval)$, we see that $|realnumber| \leq 1 - \frac{1}{continuous}$. On the other hand, by applying Corollary~2 to $(transienttwo(outputval) - transientone(outputval))/outputval^{continuous-1}$, we see that $|realnumber| \geq 1 - \frac{1}{continuous+2}$, contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:} Elkies also reports that this problem is his submission, dating back to 2005 and arising from work of Joe Harris.
It dates back further to Example 3.7 in: Hajime Kaji,
On the tangentially degenerate curves,
\textit{J. London Math. Soc. (2)} \textbf{33} (1986), 430--440, in which the second solution is given.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Elkies points out a mild generalization which may be treated using the first solution but not the second: for integers $changeling < alphaaxis < betaaxis < deltaaxis$ and $realnumber \in \CC$
which is neither zero nor a root of unity, the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
changeling & alphaaxis & betaaxis & deltaaxis \\
realnumber^{changeling} & realnumber^{alphaaxis} & realnumber^{betaaxis} & realnumber^{deltaaxis}
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has rank 3 (the problem at hand being the case $changeling=0, alphaaxis=1, betaaxis=continuous+1, deltaaxis=discrete+1$).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It seems likely that the individual polynomials $transienttri(outputval)$ are all irreducible, but this appears difficult to prove.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by David Feldman)
Note that
\[
transientsum(outputval)(1-outputval) = 1 + outputval + \cdots + outputval^{limitless-1} - limitless\, outputval^{limitless}.
\]
If $|realnumber| \geq 1$, then
\[
limitless|realnumber|^{limitless} \geq |realnumber|^{limitless-1} + \cdots + 1 \geq |realnumber^{limitless-1} + \cdots + 1|,
\]
with the first equality occurring only if $|realnumber| = 1$ and the second equality occurring only if $realnumber$ is a positive real number. Hence the equation $transientsum(realnumber)(1-realnumber) = 0$ has no solutions  with $|realnumber| \geq 1$ other than the trivial solution $realnumber=1$. Since
\[
transientsum(outputval)(1-outputval)^2 = 1 - (limitless+1) outputval^{limitless} + limitless\, outputval^{limitless+1},
\]
it now suffices to check that the curves
\[
straightline_{limitless} = \{ realnumber \in \CC: 0 < |realnumber| < 1, \left| realnumber \right|^{limitless} \left| limitless+1 - realnumber\, limitless \right| = 1 \}
\]
are pairwise disjoint as $limitless$ varies over positive integers.

Write $realnumber = imaginary+ihorizontal$; we may assume without loss of generality that $horizontal \geq 0$.
Define the function
\[
disorderfunc_{realnumber}(limitless) = limitless \log |realnumber| + \log |limitless+1-realnumber\, limitless|.
\]
One computes that for $limitless \in \RR$, $disorderfunc_{realnumber}''(limitless) < 0$ if and only if
\[
\frac{imaginary-horizontal-1}{(1-imaginary)^2 + horizontal^2} < limitless <
\frac{imaginary+horizontal-1}{(1-imaginary)^2 + horizontal^2}.
\]
In addition, $disorderfunc_{realnumber}(0) = 0$ and 
\[
disorderfunc_{realnumber}'(0) = \frac{1}{2} \log (imaginary^2+horizontal^2) + (1-imaginary) \geq \log (imaginary) + 1 - imaginary \geq 0
\]
since $\log(imaginary)$ is concave. From this, it follows that the equation $disorderfunc_{realnumber}(limitless) = 0$
can have at most one solution with $limitless>0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The reader may notice a strong similarity between this solution and the first solution. The primary difference is we compute that $disorderfunc'_{realnumber}(0) \geq 0$ instead of discovering that $disorderfunc_{realnumber}(-1) = 0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It is also possible to solve this problem using a $p$-adic valuation on the field of algebraic numbers in place of the complex absolute value; however, this leads to a substantially more complicated solution. In lieu of including such a solution here,
we refer to the approach described by Victor Wang 
here: \url{http://www.artofproblemsolving.com/Forum/viewtopic.php?f=80&t=616731}.

garbled_string.question:
Let
\[
febmlxcy(elzncuqa) = 1 + 2 elzncuqa  + 3 elzncuqa^2 + \cdots + avpjsmrd \, elzncuqa^{avpjsmrd-1}.
\]
Prove that the polynomials $wgnopzlc(elzncuqa)$
and $tbjdrxsu(elzncuqa)$ are relatively prime
for all positive integers $roczwuyt$ and $hesuvmnl$ with $roczwuyt \neq hesuvmnl$.
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose to the contrary that there exist positive integers $lovmsqni \neq roczwuyt$ and a complex number $mofjatiy$ such that $lrkabnvq(mofjatiy) = wgnopzlc(mofjatiy) = 0$. Note that $mofjatiy$ cannot be a nonnegative real number or else $lrkabnvq(mofjatiy), wgnopzlc(mofjatiy) > 0$; we may put $kxgmrpbo = mofjatiy^{-1} \neq 0,1$. For $avpjsmrd \in \{lovmsqni+1,roczwuyt+1\}$ we compute that
\[
kxgmrpbo^{avpjsmrd} = avpjsmrd\, kxgmrpbo - avpjsmrd + 1,
\qquad \overline{kxgmrpbo}^{avpjsmrd} =  avpjsmrd\, \overline{kxgmrpbo} - avpjsmrd + 1;
\]
note crucially that these equations also hold for $avpjsmrd \in \{0,1\}$.
Therefore, the function $ycstdkhr: [0, +\infty) \to \RR$ given by
\[
ycstdkhr(ewfzogud) = \left| kxgmrpbo \right|^{2ewfzogud} - ewfzogud^2 \left| kxgmrpbo \right|^2 + 2\,ewfzogud(ewfzogud-1)\mathrm{Re}(kxgmrpbo) - (ewfzogud-1)^2
\]
satisfies $ycstdkhr(ewfzogud) = 0$ for $ewfzogud \in \{0,1,lovmsqni+1,roczwuyt+1\}$. On the other hand, for all $ewfzogud \geq 0$ we have
\[
ycstdkhr'''(ewfzogud) = (2 \log \left| kxgmrpbo \right|)^3 \left| kxgmrpbo \right|^{2ewfzogud} > 0,
\]
so by Rolle's theorem, the equation $ycstdkhr^{(3-hesuvmnl)}(ewfzogud) = 0$ has at most $hesuvmnl$ distinct solutions for $hesuvmnl=0,1,2,3$. This yields the desired contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
By similar reasoning, an equation of the form $e^{elzncuqa} = P(elzncuqa)$ in which $P$ is a real polynomial of degree $jkvrtsme$ has at most $jkvrtsme+1$ real solutions. This turns out to be closely related to a concept in mathematical logic known as \emph{o-minimality}, which in turn has deep consequences for the solution of Diophantine equations.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Noam Elkies)
We recall a result commonly known as the \emph{Enestr"om-Kakeya theorem}.
\begin{lemma}
Let 
\[
ycstdkhr(elzncuqa) = oxmvarcp + ujqeslmd elzncuqa + \cdots + ypfqzavo elzncuqa^{avpjsmrd}
\]
be a polynomial with real coefficients such that $0 < oxmvarcp \leq ujqeslmd \leq \cdots \leq ypfqzavo$.
Then every root $mofjatiy \in \CC$ of $ycstdkhr$ satisfies $|mofjatiy| \leq 1$.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
If $ycstdkhr(mofjatiy) = 0$, then we may rearrange the equality $0 = ycstdkhr(mofjatiy)(mofjatiy-1)$ to obtain
\[
ypfqzavo \, mofjatiy^{avpjsmrd+1} = (ypfqzavo - smydefot) \, mofjatiy^{avpjsmrd} + \cdots + (ujqeslmd - oxmvarcp) \, mofjatiy + oxmvarcp.
\]
But if $|mofjatiy| > 1$, then 
\[
|ypfqzavo \, mofjatiy^{avpjsmrd+1}| \leq (|ypfqzavo - smydefot| + \cdots + |ujqeslmd - oxmvarcp|) |mofjatiy|^{avpjsmrd}
\leq |ypfqzavo \, mofjatiy^{avpjsmrd}|,
\]
contradiction.
\end{proof}
\begin{cor}
Let 
\[
ycstdkhr(elzncuqa) = oxmvarcp + ujqeslmd elzncuqa + \cdots + ypfqzavo elzncuqa^{avpjsmrd}
\]
be a polynomial with positive real coefficients. Then every root $mofjatiy \in \CC$ of $ycstdkhr$ satisfies $qvnefalx \leq |mofjatiy| \leq njlsavow$ for
\begin{align*}
qvnefalx &= \min\{oxmvarcp/ujqeslmd, \dots, smydefot/ypfqzavo\} \\
njlsavow &= \max\{oxmvarcp/ujqeslmd, \dots, smydefot/ypfqzavo\}.
\end{align*}
\end{cor}
\begin{proof}
The bound $|mofjatiy| \leq njlsavow$ follows by applying the lemma to the polynomial $ycstdkhr(elzncuqa/njlsavow)$.
The bound $|mofjatiy| \geq qvnefalx$ follows by applying the lemma to the reverse of the polynomial $ycstdkhr(elzncuqa/qvnefalx)$.
\end{proof}
Suppose now that $lrkabnvq(mofjatiy) = wgnopzlc(mofjatiy) = 0$ for some $mofjatiy \in \CC$ and some integers $lovmsqni < roczwuyt$. 
We clearly cannot have $roczwuyt = lovmsqni+1$, as then $lrkabnvq(0) \neq 0$ and so $wgnopzlc(mofjatiy) - lrkabnvq(mofjatiy) = (lovmsqni+1) \, mofjatiy^{lovmsqni} \neq 0$; we thus have $roczwuyt-lovmsqni \geq 2$.
By applying Corollary~2 to $lrkabnvq(elzncuqa)$, we see that $|mofjatiy| \leq 1 - \frac{1}{lovmsqni}$. On the other hand, by applying Corollary~2 to $(wgnopzlc(elzncuqa) - lrkabnvq(elzncuqa))/elzncuqa^{lovmsqni-1}$, we see that $|mofjatiy| \geq 1 - \frac{1}{lovmsqni+2}$, contradiction.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:} Elkies also reports that this problem is his submission, dating back to 2005 and arising from work of Joe Harris.
It dates back further to Example 3.7 in: Hajime Kaji,
On the tangentially degenerate curves,
\textit{J. London Math. Soc. (2)} \textbf{33} (1986), 430--440, in which the second solution is given.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
Elkies points out a mild generalization which may be treated using the first solution but not the second: for integers $zhkgsqwu<nhokiwse<exptqvyr<jkvrtsme$ and $mofjatiy \in \CC$
which is neither zero nor a root of unity, the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
zhkgsqwu & nhokiwse & exptqvyr & jkvrtsme \\
mofjatiy^{zhkgsqwu} & mofjatiy^{nhokiwse} & mofjatiy^{exptqvyr} & mofjatiy^{jkvrtsme}
\end{pmatrix}
\]
has rank 3 (the problem at hand being the case $zhkgsqwu=0, nhokiwse=1, exptqvyr=lovmsqni+1, jkvrtsme=roczwuyt+1$).

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It seems likely that the individual polynomials $febmlxcy(elzncuqa)$ are all irreducible, but this appears difficult to prove.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution:}
(by David Feldman)
Note that
\[
febmlxcy(elzncuqa)(1-elzncuqa) = 1 + elzncuqa + \cdots + elzncuqa^{avpjsmrd-1} - avpjsmrd\, elzncuqa^{avpjsmrd}.
\]
If $|mofjatiy| \geq 1$, then
\[
avpjsmrd |mofjatiy|^{avpjsmrd} \geq |mofjatiy|^{avpjsmrd-1} + \cdots + 1 \geq |mofjatiy^{avpjsmrd-1} + \cdots + 1|,
\]
with the first equality occurring only if $|mofjatiy| = 1$ and the second equality occurring only if $mofjatiy$ is a positive real number. Hence the equation $febmlxcy(mofjatiy)(1-mofjatiy) = 0$ has no solutions  with $|mofjatiy| \geq 1$ other than the trivial solution $mofjatiy=1$. Since
\[
febmlxcy(elzncuqa)(1-elzncuqa)^2 = 1 - (avpjsmrd+1) elzncuqa^{avpjsmrd} + avpjsmrd\, elzncuqa^{avpjsmrd+1},
\]
it now suffices to check that the curves
\[
pwhqostu = \{ mofjatiy \in \CC: 0 < |mofjatiy| < 1, | mofjatiy |^{avpjsmrd} | avpjsmrd+1 - mofjatiy\, avpjsmrd | = 1 \}
\]
are pairwise disjoint as $avpjsmrd$ varies over positive integers.

Write $mofjatiy = habslqyt+idjwprken$; we may assume without loss of generality that $djwprken \geq 0$.
Define the function
\[
ghufyrat_{mofjatiy}(avpjsmrd) = avpjsmrd \log |mofjatiy| + \log |avpjsmrd+1-mofjatiy\, avpjsmrd|.
\]
One computes that for $avpjsmrd \in \RR$, $ \frac{\partial^2}{\partial avpjsmrd^2} ghufyrat_{mofjatiy}(avpjsmrd) < 0$ if and only if
\[
\frac{habslqyt-djwprken-1}{(1-habslqyt)^2 + djwprken^2} < avpjsmrd <
\frac{habslqyt+djwprken-1}{(1-habslqyt)^2 + djwprken^2}.
\]
In addition, $ghufyrat_{mofjatiy}(0) = 0$ and 
\[
\frac{\partial}{\partial avpjsmrd} ghufyrat_{mofjatiy}(0) = \frac{1}{2} \log (habslqyt^2+djwprken^2) + (1-habslqyt) \geq \log (habslqyt) + 1 - habslqyt \geq 0
\]
since $\log(habslqyt)$ is concave. From this, it follows that the equation $ghufyrat_{mofjatiy}(avpjsmrd) = 0$
can have at most one solution with $avpjsmrd>0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The reader may notice a strong similarity between this solution and the first solution. The primary difference is we compute that $(\partial/\partial avpjsmrd) ghufyrat_{mofjatiy}(0) \geq 0$ instead of discovering that $ghufyrat_{mofjatiy}(-1) = 0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
It is also possible to solve this problem using a $p$-adic valuation on the field of algebraic numbers in place of the complex absolute value; however, this leads to a substantially more complicated solution. In lieu of including such a solution here,
we refer to the approach described by Victor Wang 
here: \url{http://www.artofproblemsolving.com/Forum/viewtopic.php?f=80&t=616731}.

kernel_variant.question:
For every positive integer n define the polynomial

Q_n(x)=\sum_{k=1}^{n}(3k-1)x^{k-1}=2+5x+8x^{2}+\dots +(3n-1)x^{n-1} \in \mathbb{Q}[x].

(Prototypes: Q_1(x)=2,\; Q_2(x)=2+5x,\; Q_3(x)=2+5x+8x^{2},\dots)

Prove that for any two distinct positive integers j and k the polynomials Q_j(x) and Q_k(x) are relatively prime in \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\); that is, their greatest common divisor is 1.
kernel_variant.solution:
We shall show that the only common divisor of Q_i(x) and Q_j(x) (i<j) in \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is the constant polynomial 1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Preliminaries: the Enestrom-Kakeya lemma
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let
  f(x)=a_0+a_1x+\dots +a_mx^{m}\quad (0<a_0\le a_1\le\dots \le a_m).
Then every complex root z of f satisfies
  \min_{1\le t\le m}\frac{a_{t-1}}{a_t}\;\le |z|\;\le\;\max_{1\le t\le m}\frac{a_{t-1}}{a_t}.
We shall use this twice, once to obtain an upper bound coming from Q_i and once to obtain a lower bound coming from Q_j-Q_i.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 0.  Two easy special cases
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(i)  i=1.  Here Q_1(x)=2 is a non-zero constant, so it shares no root with any other polynomial.  Hence gcd(Q_1,Q_j)=1 for every j>1.

(ii)  j=i+1 (i\ge 1).  We have
     Q_{i+1}(x)-Q_i(x)=(3(i+1)-1)x^{i}= (3i+2)x^{i}.
Any common root z of Q_i and Q_{i+1} would satisfy z^{i}=0, i.e. z=0, but Q_i(0)=2\neq 0.  Thus Q_i and Q_{i+1} are coprime.

From now on we assume
     2\le i<j,\qquad j-i\ge 2,
and seek a contradiction from the assumption that the two polynomials have a common non-zero root z.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  An upper bound for |z| coming from Q_i
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Write
   Q_i(x)=b_0+b_1x+\dots +b_{i-1}x^{i-1},\qquad b_t=3t+2.
The coefficients are strictly increasing, so the Enestrom-Kakeya lemma gives
   |z|\le R_i:=\max_{1\le t\le i-1}\frac{b_{t-1}}{b_t}=\frac{b_{i-2}}{b_{i-1}}
           =\frac{3(i-2)+2}{3(i-1)+2}=\frac{3i-4}{3i-1}.\tag{1}
Because i\ge 2, this is a positive number smaller than 1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  A lower bound for |z| coming from Q_j-Q_i
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Since Q_i and Q_j have the same first i terms, the smallest exponent occurring in Q_j(x)-Q_i(x) is i.  Factor x^{i}:
  Q_j(x)-Q_i(x)=x^{i}\,S(x),
where
  S(x)=\sum_{t=1}^{j-i}\bigl(3(i+t)-1\bigr)x^{t-1}.
Denote the coefficients of S by
  c_{t-1}=3(i+t)-1\quad(1\le t\le j-i).
Again the coefficients are strictly increasing, so Enestrom-Kakeya yields
  |z|\ge r_i:=\min_{1\le t\le j-i-1}\frac{c_{t-1}}{c_t}=\frac{c_0}{c_1}
        =\frac{3(i+1)-1}{3(i+2)-1}=\frac{3i+2}{3i+5}.\tag{2}
Note that S has degree at least 1 because j-i\ge 2, so the lemma applies.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Incompatibility of the two bounds
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Comparing (1) and (2) we compute
  (3i+2)(3i-1)-(3i-4)(3i+5)=18>0\;\;\;(i\ge 2),
hence
  r_i=\frac{3i+2}{3i+5}>\frac{3i-4}{3i-1}=R_i.
Thus the lower bound for |z| coming from Q_j-Q_i exceeds the upper bound coming from Q_i.  No complex number can satisfy both inequalities, so Q_i and Q_j have no common non-zero root, contradicting the assumption.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
All possible cases have been covered: when i=1 or j=i+1 the polynomials are coprime by the easy arguments in Step 0, and when 2\le i<j with j-i\ge 2 they cannot share a root by Steps 1-3.  Therefore every pair (Q_j,Q_k) with j\neq k is relatively prime in \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\).

core_steps: Apply Eneström–Kakeya to any polynomial with positive, non-decreasing coefficients to get the annulus  r ≤ |z| ≤ R  where  r = min a_{k-1}/a_k ,  R = max a_{k-1}/a_k .
Assume two different indices  i < j  share a root  z :  P_i(z)=P_j(z)=0 .
Note the trivial case  j=i+1 :  P_j-P_i = (i+1)x^{i} ≠0  at such a root, so force  j-i≥2 .
Compute the two EK–bounds:  |z| ≤ 1−1/i  from P_i  and  |z| ≥ 1−1/(i+2)  from (P_j−P_i)/x^{i−1}; these are incompatible.

slot1.description: Exact list 1,2,…,n of coefficients.  The proof only needs them to be positive and non-decreasing so any such sequence works.

slot2.description: Global scalar multiplying each P_n(x).  Zeros (and hence relative primeness) are unchanged if every polynomial is multiplied by the same non-zero constant.

slot3.description: Lower-degree‐shift used to avoid the j=i+1 case.  Any fixed gap δ≥2 (forcing j−i≥δ) yields bounds whose overlap is impossible, so ‘+2’ can be replaced by ‘+δ’.

slot4.description: Use of the usual complex absolute value.  Any absolute value (Archimedean or non-Archimedean) for which an Eneström–Kakeya analogue holds could replace it.
